title: Before You Arrive
text:
Note



These materials help participants from both communities build enough background to engage productively from Day 1. You do not need to be an expert in the other field — the goal is familiarity with key concepts and terminology.

title: Before You Arrive
text:
Pre-Course Reading

For Participants from Survival Analysis
These readings introduce infectious disease modeling concepts used throughout the workshop.

  
    
    Author et al. (Year)Short title
  


Key concepts to be familiar with:

The SIR/SEIRD compartmental model framework
The basic reproduction number R0 and effective reproduction number Reff(t)
Reporting delays and underreporting in epidemic surveillance data
The difference between individual-level and aggregate (population-level) data




For Participants from Infectious Disease Modeling
These readings introduce survival analysis concepts and methods used throughout the workshop.

  
    
    Author et al. (Year)Short title
  

Key concepts to be familiar with:

The hazard function and its relationship to survival probability
Right-censoring and interval-censoring: what they mean and how they are handled
Competing risks: what they are and why ignoring them can bias estimates
Multi-state models: modeling transitions between multiple states (e.g., S → E → I → R)
Frailty models: incorporating unobserved heterogeneity




Cross-disciplinary Reading

  
    
    Author et al. (Year)Short title

title: Before You Arrive
text:
Software
The workshop uses R as the primary computing environment. Please install the following before arriving.

R and RStudio

R (≥ 4.3.0 recommended)
RStudio Desktop



R Packages

Survival Analysis
install.packages(c(
  "survival",    # core survival analysis
  "survminer",   # visualization
  "mstate",      # multi-state models
  "flexsurv",    # flexible parametric survival models
  "frailtyEM",   # frailty models
  "timereg",     # additive hazard models
  "cmprsk"       # competing risks
))


Infectious Disease Modeling
install.packages(c(
  "EpiEstim",    # reproduction number estimation
  "EpiNow2",     # nowcasting and R estimation with delays
  "deSolve",     # ODE solvers for compartmental models
  "odin",        # fast ODE model specification
  "socialmixr"   # contact matrices for structured populations
))


General
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "ggplot2",
  "patchwork",
  "knitr",
  "rmarkdown"
))
Verify your installation:
library(survival)
library(mstate)
library(EpiEstim)
library(deSolve)
cat("All core packages loaded successfully.\n")

title: Before You Arrive
text:
Case Study Data
The two case study datasets will be provided to participants ahead of the workshop and posted here for download.






Warning



Please do not share the case study data externally — the complete underlying dataset is revealed at the end of the workshop as part of the learning experience.


See the Case Studies page for full descriptions of both datasets.

title: Case Studies
text:
The workshop is structured around two case studies derived from a single simulated population-wide outbreak. The underlying data are inspired by the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic and generated from a structured compartmental SEIRD model in a closed population of 1,000,000 individuals, with heterogeneity in susceptibility and infectiousness, an intervention period, and a vaccination campaign introduced partway through the outbreak.






Important



Participants do not see the underlying complete individual-level dataset until the very end of the workshop. The two case study datasets are extracts designed to mimic realistic data availability settings.

title: Case Studies
text:
The workshop is structured around two case studies derived from a single simulated population-wide outbreak. The underlying data are inspired by the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic and generated from a structured compartmental SEIRD model in a closed population of 1,000,000 individuals, with heterogeneity in susceptibility and infectiousness, an intervention period, and a vaccination campaign introduced partway through the outbreak.






Important



Participants do not see the underlying complete individual-level dataset until the very end of the workshop. The two case study datasets are extracts designed to mimic realistic data availability settings.

title: Case Studies
section: Case Study A — Natural Course of the Epidemic
text:
Case Study A — Natural Course of the Epidemic
Focus period: Before vaccination becomes available.
Available data: Daily aggregate counts of new infections (subject to reporting delays and underreporting), hospitalizations, and deaths.
Analytical aims:

Estimate the reproduction number (R0 and Reff) over time
Estimate the effect of intervention measures on transmission
Predict the future course of the epidemic under different scenarios
Handle incomplete data — censoring, reporting delays, and underreporting

title: Case Studies
text:
Case Study B — Vaccine Efficacy
Focus period: After vaccination becomes available.
Available data: Daily aggregate counts of infections, hospitalizations, deaths, and vaccinations (including uptake aggregated over population subgroups).
Analytical aims:

Estimate vaccine efficacy (VE) against infection, hospitalization, and death
Understand time-dependent vaccine effectiveness (waning immunity)
Characterize heterogeneity in vaccine uptake and efficacy across subgroups

title: Case Studies
text:
Working Groups
Participants are divided into four mixed-discipline groups (two per case study), intentionally composed across SA and IDM expertise and career stage. Groups working on the same case study meet on Day 2 to compare analysis plans before refining independently. All groups share findings in the daily plenary sessions.

title: Group Analysis Scripts
text: Each working group has a subfolder here for their analysis scripts. Groups are free to organize their code however works best for them — the structure below is just a starting point.

title: Group Analysis Scripts
text:
Guidelines

Use descriptive filenames prefixed with a number (e.g., 01_, 02_) to indicate order
Add a short comment at the top of each script explaining what it does
Commit regularly so your group members have access to the latest version
If a script takes a long time to run, save output objects (.rds files) and commit those too

title: Vignettes
text: Vignettes document the analytical approaches developed during the workshop, combining scientific motivation, step-by-step methodology, and fully reproducible R code. Each vignette covers a specific SA–IDM integration example applied to one of the two case studies.

title: Vignettes
text:
How to Contribute a Vignette

Create a new .qmd file in the vignettes/ folder using the naming convention vignette-[topic].qmd
Write your vignette using the template below
Commit and push — it will appear on the site automatically


Vignette Template
---
title: "Your Vignette Title"
author: "Author Names"
date: today
---

## Scientific Background

## Methods

## Data

## Analysis

## Results

## Discussion and Limitations

## Session Info

title: Connecting Survival Analysis and Infectious Disease Modeling
text:
15 – 19 June 2026  ·  Lorentz Center @lambda, Leiden, the Netherlands
Part of the NIAS-Lorentz Program

title: Connecting Survival Analysis and Infectious Disease Modeling
text:
About the Workshop
Survival analysis (SA) and infectious disease modelling (IDM) are two disciplines that frequently tackle similar scientific problems—time-to-event outcomes, heterogeneity in populations, and the effects of interventions—yet they do so with largely separate methodological frameworks. This workshop brings together researchers from both communities to make these (dis)connections explicit, learn from one another, and develop shared tools for cross-disciplinary analysis.
Over five days, participants will work in small mixed groups on two shared case studies using realistic simulated epidemic data, and collectively produce vignettes, code examples, and a “Ten Simple Rules” guide for SA–IDM integration. See the Programme for the full schedule.
